# Bootstrapping an Architecture 2.0 Loop {#sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This appendix gives a practical path for building a small credible Architecture 2.0 loop without turning the book into a tool manual. The first useful loop should be bounded, inspectable, rejectable, and tied to an accountable decision.}
```

The smallest useful Architecture 2.0 loop is not "install an AI assistant." It
is an explicit loop with a task, representation, tool wrapper, method role,
evidence standard, and accountable accept/reject decision. The first loop should be
small enough to inspect, cheap enough to run repeatedly, and constrained
enough that failure is informative.

> **Bootstrap loop.** A bootstrap loop is the smallest credible
> Architecture 2.0 loop. It requires one bounded task, one representation, one tool
> interface, one method role, one evidence standard, and one accountable
> decision owner.

The goal of this appendix is to help a reader start without overbuilding. A
large automated research harness may eventually include many tools, critics,
planners, datasets, dashboards, and review steps. That is not the first move.
The first move is a bounded loop that can produce a review record another architect
can read.
For a student or new researcher, a finished bootstrap loop with a readable record
is already a serious artifact, even if the AI role inside it is modest.

The bootstrap pattern is deliberately minimal (@fig-bootstrap-loop).
Choose one task, one representation, one tool wrapper, one method
role, one set of evidence and rejection gates, and one decision owner. If that
small loop cannot reject a result, a larger version will only hide the
problem.

![**A first Architecture 2.0 loop should be small and rejectable.** The loop should be bounded, inspectable, rejectable, and tied to an accountable decision. It can grow only after the task, representation, tool interface, method role, evidence checks, and decision rights are visible.](images/F12-bootstrap-loop){#fig-bootstrap-loop width="100%" fig-alt="Six-step bootstrap loop showing a bounded task, representation, tool wrapping, method-role assignment, evidence checks, review record, and accountable decision."}

## The Architecture Loop Playbook

If an engineer is asked how to incorporate AI into an architecture or hardware/software co-design
loop, the first answer is not to pick a model. The first answer is to turn
one part of the work into a bounded, represented, rejectable loop. AI then
has a method role inside that loop. It may generate, predict, search,
summarize, critique, verify, or coordinate, but it does not own the
architecture commitment.

:::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before adding an AI role, name the decision and its loop. Define the candidate space,
the tool feedback, the evidence path, what may reject the result, and the
accountable role or process that accepts or escalates it. Only then choose the narrow AI role that
makes that loop cheaper, broader, faster, or more inspectable without weakening
the evidence standard.
::::

Instead of asking where to add AI, an engineer should ask which
architecture decision is bottlenecked by representation, search, prediction,
review, or evidence. A compact playbook for that decision appears in @tbl-silicon-playbook.

| **Engineer move** | **Represent** | **AI role to allow** | **Gate before trust** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bound the decision | Workload slice, objective, non-goals, and hard constraints. | Summarizer or planner that exposes missing state. | Reject if the task cannot fail visibly. |
| Expose the candidate space | Knobs, legal actions, invalid states, and prior rejections. | Generator or searcher constrained to legal moves. | Reject outputs that invent actions, interfaces, or assumptions. |
| Wrap the feedback | Tool versions, configs, seeds, cost, latency, and failure logs. | Tool caller or coordinator. | Reject runs without provenance or records of failure. |
| Choose the fidelity ladder | Proxy, replay, simulation, synthesis, emulation, or deployment evidence. | Predictor, surrogate, or active learner. | Escalate when the claim or commitment exceeds proxy authority. |
| Review the result | Alternatives, sensitivity, uncertainty, and rejected regions. | Critic, verifier, or explanation generator. | Reject if the result cannot explain what would change the decision. |
| Commit or revise | Accountable acceptance, escalation, or rollback decision, including any authority delegated to automated checks. | Decision support or bounded automated disposition. | The method cannot expand its own authority or waive a failed constraint. |

: **The architecture loop playbook starts from decisions, not tools.** Each step asks what an engineer should represent, what AI role is legitimate, and what gate prevents a plausible output from becoming an unsupported architecture commitment. {#tbl-silicon-playbook .playbook-table tbl-colwidths="[18,28,25,29]"}

This is deliberately smaller than an enterprise AI strategy. It is an
architecture loop playbook because it treats architecture work as coupled to
tools, costs, constraints, evidence, and irreversible commitments. A team can repeat it for
an accelerator search, a memory-hierarchy study, a compiler/runtime option, a
benchmark update, or a verification triage task, but the same rule holds. Make
the loop explicit before giving the method more authority.

The rest of this appendix walks the same discipline step by step, following the
loop in @fig-bootstrap-loop; the playbook cuts it by decision, the sections
below cut it by build step.

## Choose a Bounded Task

Start with a task where success and failure can be inspected. Good first
tasks include a small design-space exploration, workload characterization,
configuration search, benchmark generation, design review, or report
critique. Avoid starting with "design a processor" or "automate the flow."
Those are too large to debug.

A bounded task has three properties. First, the input is known, such as a workload
slice, design question, simulator configuration, benchmark version, or review
packet. Second, the output is inspectable, whether it is a ranked list, plot, rejected
candidate set, supporting evidence record, critique, or recommendation. Third, failure is
useful. If the loop gives a bad answer, the record explains whether the problem
was the task, representation, tool wrapper, method, evidence, or human
instruction.

For the lighthouse prompt, the first task should not be the whole mobile XR
compute subsystem. A better first task might characterize a slice of XRBench, an extended reality benchmark suite, and compare three compute-organization candidates. The candidates are a vector CPU
extension, an intentionally separated accelerator candidate used to expose interface costs, and a shared-memory System-on-Chip (SoC)
block. The output is a latency-energy-power comparison with rejected
alternatives and supporting evidence. That is still hard, but it is a loop
rather than a wish. It uses
the same bounded slice that @sec-running-the-loop walks with constructed values,
so the first loop you build can borrow the chapter's structure. Its evidence,
however, must come from your own runs. The
[companion labs](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/tree/main/labs) provide a
separate executable practice path and may produce a different result.

## Choose a Representation

The representation is what the loop can see and change. A minimal
representation may include configuration files, workload traces, simulator
outputs, architecture descriptions, scripts, plots, notes, constraints, and
prior rejected candidates. It does not have to be perfect. It does have to be
explicit.

Write down four boundaries before running anything:

- What the loop may read.
- What the loop may write.
- What the loop must not change.
- What assumptions live outside the representation.

The last item matters. Early loops often fail because important state is
outside the loop, such as a simulator default, a benchmark version, an undocumented
constraint, a hidden preprocessing step, a fragile script, or a human judgment
that never gets recorded. For example, if an AI generates an accelerator configuration but the area constraint is held only in the architect's head, the AI can propose invalid, oversized designs. Exposing that missing constraint is a success of the loop, not a failure of the model. Those gaps are not embarrassing. They are exactly
what the bootstrap loop is meant to expose.

## Wrap the Environment

An environment is more than a command that returns a number. It defines the
actions the loop can take, the observations it receives, the constraints it
must obey, the cost of each evaluation, and the provenance recorded for each
run.

For a first wrapper, keep the interface narrow:

- a small action space, such as a few tunable architecture parameters;
- a fixed workload or small workload set;
- explicit invalid-action checks;
- one low-fidelity metric and one higher-fidelity check;
- logged tool versions, seeds, configurations, and errors;
- a run directory that preserves successful and failed attempts.

The wrapper should make failure visible. A hard-constraint violation can reject
a candidate. A compile error, timeout, stale benchmark, or incomplete log should
first be recorded as an environment failure unless a declared rule makes
the candidate responsible for that outcome. For example, if a candidate with a
256~KB Level 2 (L2) cache and 128-bit vector unit, run at seed 7 under simulator
v2.3, times out after ten minutes, the log should preserve the timeout and its
context. It becomes a candidate rejection only if the loop declared that limit
as part of the candidate contract. A first environment that records failures is
more valuable than a larger environment that only reports successes.

## Assign the Method Role

Do not begin by asking a model or automated optimizer to do everything. Choose one method
role and make it explicit. The role might be generator, searcher, predictor,
summarizer, critic, planner, tool caller, verifier, or coordinator.

The role should match the task and feedback budget. If evaluations are cheap,
a search or optimization role may be reasonable. If evaluations are expensive,
a critic, summarizer, or surrogate predictor may be more useful. If the
representation is messy, the first useful role may be extraction and
organization, not optimization. If the tool wrapper is fragile, the first role
may be a verifier that checks whether runs are valid.

A useful rule is to write the method sentence before implementing the method:

> This system will act as a *role* that takes *inputs*, is allowed to
> perform *actions*, receives *feedback*, and produces
> *evidence for a declared decision process*.

If that sentence cannot be completed, the loop is not ready for method work.

## Predeclare Evidence and Decision Rules

Before the first run, state what evidence is enough, what evidence is not
enough, and what forces rejection or escalation. This is the smallest version
of the trust discipline in @sec-feedback-verification-trust.

For example:

- A proxy estimate can rank candidates but cannot justify a design
   conclusion.
- A simulator result is valid only if the workload version, seed,
   configuration, and tool version are logged.
- A candidate is rejected if it violates a hard constraint. A failed run is
   recorded as an environment failure, while a multiobjective tradeoff remains
   for the declared decision rule or accountable review.
- A result escalates to higher fidelity only after it passes the low-cost
   checks and preserves its assumptions.
- Any claim that changes the commitment level must pass through a declared
   decision right. Routine checks may exercise delegated authority, while
   exceptions, waivers, and consequential transitions remain accountable.

Rejection authority is not pessimism. It is what makes automation useful.
Without rejection, the loop can only produce artifacts. With relevant
observations, provenance, scope, and acceptance or rejection criteria, it can
produce evidence.

## Fill in the Minimal Design-Loop Card

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full design-loop card and review
rubric. For a first bootstrap pass, transfer the outputs from
@tbl-silicon-playbook into the card before running the loop, then revise the
same card after the first run. Do not create a second checklist. Three questions
are enough to test whether the first card is ready.

1. What bounded claim or decision is this loop meant to support?
2. What may each participant read, change, test, reject, or escalate?
3. What failed result would stop the loop, and who may authorize the next step
   or a waiver?

The lighthouse instance below shows those questions applied to one decision.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{30\baselineskip}
```

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="Bound one XR decision before automating it" icon=false}
**Context.** A team has product-level intent, "improve mobile XR efficiency," but
no bounded loop, and is tempted to ask a model to design the subsystem.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** Use "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
to fix one workload slice and deadline, "3~W Thermal Design Power (TDP) target" as a hard rejection
gate, and "vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or System-on-Chip (SoC) block" as the candidate set.
Keep "64-bit RISC-V-based" fixed in this first loop. Candidates must preserve
the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) and Application Binary Interface (ABI), compiler/runtime path, and software compatibility rather than
inventing a new contract.

**Loop role.** The primary AI role is search. A bounded reporting subrole
organizes latency, energy, power, and data-movement evidence, and preserves
rejected alternatives.

**Boundary.** This is not the whole subsystem; cache policy, voltage
assumptions, full memory hierarchy, physical design, and reliability evidence
are next-loop questions unless they are needed to reject a candidate now.

**Takeaway.** The declared architecture review decides which candidate, if any,
has enough evidence to carry to Register Transfer Level (RTL) implementation,
and records the accountable owner of that transition.
:::

The first pilot should fit in a short review cycle. A practical agenda for a
team, research project, or design-review group fits that cycle
(@tbl-first-pilot-agenda).

| **Pilot step** | **Output** | **Timebox** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Pick one decision | A single architecture question with an owner and a non-goal. | 15 minutes. |
| Draft Level 0 | Intent, bounded task, and legal, invalid, and deferred design choices. | 30 minutes. |
| Run one cheap check | One proxy, replay, compile, simulator, or review result with provenance. | One afternoon or less. |
| Preserve one failure | A rejected candidate, invalid action, missing input, or tool error with reason. | During the first run. |
| Review commitment | A note that says whether the evidence supports exploration, implementation, escalation, or rejection. | 15 minutes. |

: **A first pilot should be small enough to finish.** The goal is to create one
readable record with a success, a failure, and a bounded decision, not to build a
complete agentic platform. {#tbl-first-pilot-agenda tbl-colwidths="[22,46,22]"}

After the first run, evaluate the loop's effectiveness. The loop should produce
a record another architect can read and preserve both successful and failed
attempts. The generated evidence must match the commitment level, and a
rejection rule must trigger informatively when appropriate. Finally, identify
what should be revised first among the task, representation, environment,
method, evidence rule, or decision process.

If the loop fails to produce a readable record, do not add more AI participants. Make the
loop visible. If nothing in the loop could reject a result, do not trust the output. Add a rejection gate. The simplest
credible Architecture 2.0 loop is not the one with the most automation. It
is the one whose evidence and failure modes are visible enough to improve.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card turns that first run into a blank card and
review rubric that can be reused for papers, proposals, design projects, and
design reviews.
